meta.title: Wedding website setup with React and GitHub Pages - Claude

meta.name: description
meta.content: Claude is Anthropic's AI, built for problem solvers. Tackle complex challenges, analyze data, write code, and think through your hardest work.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yes, you can use a custom domain like hdgwedding.

div.p: Yes, you can use a custom domain like hdgwedding.com with GitHub Pages! You just need to own the domain (buy it from a registrar like Namecheap, Google Domains, etc.), then add a CNAME file to your repo and configure DNS records pointing to GitHub's servers. GitHub Pages has solid support for this.
Let's check your current versions first:

hr.p: A couple things worth knowing before we dive into building:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: For GitHub Pages with React/Vite, you'll want the gh-pages package and a small tweak to vite.config.js (base set to / since you're using a custom domain)
You'll also drop a CNAME file in your /public folder containing just hdgwedding.com so it survives deploys

hr.p: Once you've got the project set up and share those version numbers, we can start building the site. What's the vibe you're going for — do you have colors, a theme, any pages in mind already?

div.h2: Claude responded: That empty output just means you haven't installed anything yet — totally normal starting point.

div.p: That empty output just means you haven't installed anything yet — totally normal starting point.
On the free domain question — not really, at least not for a clean .com. Here's the honest breakdown:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: GitHub Pages default gives you yourusername.github.io/wedding — free, no purchase needed, but not a custom domain
Freenom used to offer free .tk, .ml etc. domains but they've become unreliable/sketchy and I'd avoid them for a wedding site
Some registrars offer the first year free as a promo (like Namecheap sometimes does .com for ~$1 first year)

div.p: Honest recommendation: A .com domain runs about $10-15/year from Namecheap or Cloudflare Registrar (Cloudflare sells at cost, no markup). For a wedding website that'll be up 1-2 years, that's $20-30 total — pretty reasonable.

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Check Cloudflare Registrar (cloudflare.com/products/registrar) — at-cost pricing, usually ~$9-10/year for .com
Or just use the free GitHub Pages URL: harrisonandXXX.github.io — honestly guests won't care, it still works great

hr.p: Want to just go with the free GitHub Pages URL for now and we can always add a custom domain later? That way you can start building today. What's your fiancée's name so we can figure out the URL/site name?

div.h2: Claude responded: The react --version not working is normal — React isn't a CLI tool you run directly.

div.p: The react --version not working is normal — React isn't a CLI tool you run directly. But we can see create-react-app is at 5.1.0 and from the earlier output you're on Node v22.22.0 and npm 10.9.4.
Actually though — for a modern project, skip Create React App entirely. It's outdated and slow. Vite is the move. Let's just spin up your wedding project fresh:

div.h2: Claude responded: Nope!

div.p: Nope! That's the magic of npx — npm create vite@latest downloads and runs it on the spot without needing a global install. Just run it and it'll handle itself.

div.h2: Claude responded: Exactly — the npm create vite@latest .

div.p: Exactly — the npm create vite@latest . -- --template react command sets everything up, then you just run npm install after and it pulls in React, ReactDOM, and all dependencies automatically from the generated package.json. So the full flow is just:

div.p: cool, i took notes with my fiance of all the things we want. im going to give grok the picture of the notes and organize it into a prompt for you to build out the whole site, list the biolerplate files to remove, and give me all the files to copy and paste in. what ill tell you now, the site MUST BE RESPONSIVE. if i had to guess, over 75% of people will pull this up on their phone. ill develop on my mac but we'll also need to set up a network host so i can pull it up on my phone to test. the user shouldn't have to scroll at all... i want the homepage background to be 3 different pictures (use placeholder1 , 2, 3 for now, ill manually replace later.), that basically carousel, but it will be on a timer, the user shouldn't have the option to make it change, so really like a slideshow more than carousel i guess. we'll have a navbar on the top and the footer will have nav links as well, so the user wont have to scroll anywhere to find anything. stand by for the prompt. is there anything else you need before we begin?

div.p: Love the planning! A few quick questions/notes before the prompt comes in:

Color palette / vibe (rustic, modern, floral, minimal?)
Font preferences or just pick something wedding-appropriate?

div.p: One thing to flag on "no scrolling": That works great on most pages, but if you have a lot of content (like a schedule with many events, or registry items) some pages may need to scroll internally. I'll design it so the navbar and footer are always visible and content fits the viewport — but wanted to be upfront that some pages may scroll within a content area rather than the whole page. Sound good?
For the network/phone testing, I'll include this in vite.config.js:

div.p: You are an expert wedding invitation and website designer. I need you to create a clean, elegant, and professional wedding website based on the handwritten notes in this image, with the following specific updates. Here is the full content from the handwritten planning notes, with the requested changes applied: RSVP Section: * Form should include: * First Name * Song / Favorite Couple Song * Allergies Registry: * Include a registry link Details Section: * Address * Hotel – link to website with code included * Dress Code * Parking (with parking options) Hero / Front Page: * Names: Dale and Time (likely "Tina" or "Tyme" – please use as written for now or suggest best interpretation) * Date: (placeholder) * Location: (placeholder) * Text: “RSVP | See Details | Registry” Updated / Additional Elements: * Replace “Be Tinker” with “New Gearhart coming soon!” as ticker text (a scrolling or animated marquee-style ticker at the top or bottom of the page). * Add animated text that pops up on the homepage: "Welcome to Harrison and Daelyn's wedding website! Click the links to RSVP, check out our registry, and find more details about our wedding!" Overall requirements: * Modern, elegant, and clean design * Mobile-friendly * Include sections: Hero, Details, Registry, RSVP * Professional typography and layout * Warm and romantic tone * Include an animated ticker with the text “New Gearhart coming soon!” * Include a welcoming animated pop-up text on the homepage as specified above Please create a detailed description of how the wedding website should look and function, including: * Suggested layout and section order * Color palette ideas (elegant neutrals with soft romantic accents) * Typography recommendations * How the animated ticker and pop-up text should behave * Specific content for each section * Suggestions for any missing information (names, date, location, etc.) If anything in the handwriting is unclear, please note it and offer the most logical interpretation.

RSVP form — no Last Name field was listed so I'll leave it out unless you say otherwise. Also no "number of guests" was listed — adding that since it's pretty essential for a wedding RSVP, let me know if you want it removed
Registry link — I'll use a placeholder # link, you can swap it later
Hotel code + address + dress code + parking — all placeholders you'll fill in
Ticker — I'll put "New Gearhart coming soon! 🎉" as a subtle animated strip
Welcome popup — animates in on first load, dismissable

Primary: #3F1521 (Vintage Wine) — deep, elegant
Accent: #AC5B67 (Deep Puce) — mid-tone bridge
Light accent: #E9ADCA (Lilac Sachet) — soft pink highlights
Gold: #B08C3F (Pantone 10128C) — for decorative details

div.p: On RSVP email: Emailjs is the cleanest free option for a static site — no backend needed, sends directly to your Gmail. I'll wire it up with your email. You'll just need to create a free EmailJS account and drop in your keys (I'll tell you exactly where).
Guest list on RSVP page: I'll build it so guests click their name → form pops up with name autofilled → they fill the rest → submits to your email.
Building now — this will be a full multi-file output.

div.p: continue, use 20 fake placeholder names for the rsvp, hardcoded in so i can replace later. use pagination and have a search bar with placeholder texts as direction "search guest name here". each row containing the names should highlight on mouse hover to indicate that its clickable. The registry page super simple, just some text to the effect of "we dont expect anything, we appreciate anything we get" but more classy. Button that explicitly says click here to go to registry link or somethging to that effect. feel free have the guest list data inside the rsvp file. less files easier.

hr.p: Vite will print two URLs — one for your Mac (localhost:5173) and one with your local IP (like 192.168.1.x:5173) for your phone on the same WiFi.

Create a template — set it up to receive these variables:
{{guest_name}}, {{attending}}, {{first_name}}, {{song_request}}, {{allergies}}
Copy your Service ID, Template ID, and Public Key

div.h2: Claude responded: CSS Modules are just a React pattern where each component gets its own scoped CSS file so class names don't clash with each other.

div.p: CSS Modules are just a React pattern where each component gets its own scoped CSS file so class names don't clash with each other. The .module.css extension is what tells Vite to treat it that way.
You've already seen them implemented — every component imports its own styles like this:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: Then instead of className="navbar" you write className={styles.navbar}. That's it. Vite handles the rest automatically, no extra setup needed.

The files are already written and wired up correctly in everything I gave you — you don't need to do anything special. Just copy the files into the right folders and npm install like normal. They'll just work.

The only thing that'd trip you up is if you tried to manually edit a class name in the CSS file but forgot to update the matching styles.whatever reference in the JSX, but that's the same as any CSS change.

div.h2: Claude responded: Just create a new file and name it with .

div.p: Just create a new file and name it with .module.css at the end — like Navbar.module.css. That's it. VS Code treats it like any normal CSS file, you just type regular CSS in it. The .module part is just part of the filename, nothing special to configure in VS Code.
So for each component, you'd have two files side by side:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre-wrap;

div.p: Just right-click the folder in the VS Code sidebar → New File → type the full name including .module.css.

div.h2: Claude responded: Your files are named Home.

div.p: Your files are named Home.jsx but the import is looking for Home — that part is fine, Vite resolves that automatically. The issue is the file just doesn't exist at that path yet.

Check your src/pages/ folder in VS Code — do you have Home.jsx in there? My guess is either the pages folder wasn't created yet, or the file got saved somewhere else. Share a screenshot of your src folder structure in VS Code and I can tell you exactly what's off.

Too much space between the body and navbar — shouldn't be scrollable
Add "© by Harrison Gearhart" in footer

hr.p: Before I make all these changes, two quick questions:

ol.li: "No hamburger on mobile" — so on small screens you want all nav links visible in the bar instead of collapsing to a hamburger? Just want to confirm since that can get tight on small phones.
"Harmeldon" — what is this? A venue name? A location? So I know what label to use.

div.h2: Claude responded: Got it — Harwelden is the venue, and we're keeping nav links always visible (no hamburger) even on mobile.

div.p: Got it — Harwelden is the venue, and we're keeping nav links always visible (no hamburger) even on mobile. Makes sense for the audience.
Here's every change I'm making:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Navbar: evenly spaced, H&D monogram + links always visible, no hamburger ever
Mobile nav: shrink font/padding so links fit without collapsing
Tighter body-to-navbar gap across all pages, consistent padding
Footer: add "© by Harrison Gearhart"
Font picker: make fonts more visually distinct from each other
Homepage: 4th slide slot for Harwelden, CTA links tightened underneath hero
Details: add Harwelden photo placeholder section
All page padding tightened and made consistent

div.h2: Claude responded: Got it, noted — tighten the gap between the navbar and page content, and between page content and the footer.

div.p: Got it, noted — tighten the gap between the navbar and page content, and between page content and the footer. Not the internal padding of the cards/sections themselves.

div.p: Here's what changed and what to replace in VS Code:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: src/components/Navbar.jsx + Navbar.module.css — no hamburger, always visible, shrinks gracefully on mobile
src/components/Footer.jsx — added © by Harrison Gearhart
src/components/FontPicker.jsx + FontPicker.module.css — each font now shows a description so they're easy to tell apart
src/pages/Home.jsx — 4th Harwelden slide added
src/pages/Details.jsx + Details.module.css — Harwelden photo placeholder at top, real address filled in
src/App.css — tightened the gap between navbar and page content, and between content and footer

div.p: When you're ready to drop in the real Harwelden photo, just drop the image in src/assets/, import it at the top of Details.jsx, and swap out the placeholder div for .

div.p: The file on my end is correct but it still has max-width: 1100px constraining the inner div which can cause issues. Let me fix that and write every single file fresh to outputs right now:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: All 23 files. Replace every single file in your VS Code project — don't try to merge, just select all and overwrite each one. The navbar will show all links on every screen size, no hamburger anywhere.

div.h2: You said: now its showing up on the navbar.

div.class: group relative inline-flex gap-2 bg-bg-300 rounded-xl pl-2.5 py-2.5 break-words text-text-100 transition-all max-w-[75ch] flex-col !px-4 max-w-[85%]

div.class: font-large !font-user-message grid grid-cols-1 gap-2 py-0.5 relative [&_ul]:!space-y-0 [&_ol]:!space-y-0 [&_ul]:pl-8 [&_ol]:pl-8
div.style: max-height: none; overflow: hidden; position: relative;
div.p: now its showing up on the navbar.. but i want desktop and mobile to evenly space out all of the navbar content evenly across the whole page. and the font picker is literally broken the font isnt changing when i select a new one.. and i need everypage to have a scroll to top function so the page loads at the top of the screen no matter what. got it?

div.h2: Claude responded: Got it.

div.p: Got it. 3 fixes:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Navbar — evenly space everything across full width
Font picker — broken, needs to actually apply the font
Scroll to top — every page loads at top

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Fix navbar to space evenly across full width

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Fix navbar to space evenly across full width

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now fix the font picker — the bug is that CSS variables set via document.documentElement.style.setProperty don't always force a re-render on elements using var(). The fix is to also set the font directly on document.body:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Fix App.jsx - add ScrollToTop component and fix font change to actually apply

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Fix App.jsx - add ScrollToTop component and fix font change to actually apply

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now fix the FontPicker itself — the sample text needs to actually render at a big enough size to see the difference, and the font application needs to trigger a visible change on the page title elements:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now update index.css so the font variable is applied to ALL elements including headings — this is the real root fix for why font changes weren't showing:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Only 4 files changed this round. Replace these in VS Code:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: src/App.jsx — added ScrollToTop component, fixed font change logic
src/index.css — added h1-h6 { font-family: var(--font-display) } which is why font picker wasn't working
src/components/Navbar.module.css — changed to justify-content: space-evenly on both .inner and .links
src/components/FontPicker.jsx — clean version

div.p: desktop and mobile remove the H&D from the navbar and make the font bigger for the links. will help the spacing and readability. on mobile, the scroll up feature is covering up the body title text.. fix that. the padding issue still hasn't been fixed... to be clear, the RSVP and Details pages are perfect, so make the home page and registry page match those pages please destop and mobile!!!

div.h2: Claude responded: Let me look at the current state of the relevant files first: Good, I can see all the issues clearly now.

div.p: Let me look at the current state of the relevant files first:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.p: Good, I can see all the issues clearly now. The Registry uses its own .wrapper/.inner structure instead of the shared .page/.page-content classes — that's why it doesn't match. The Home hero padding on mobile is also pushing the title behind the scroll-to-top button. Fixing everything now:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now fix Registry to use the same .page / .page-content structure as RSVP and Details:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now fix Home — the hero padding-top on mobile is what's pushing the title behind the FontPicker scroll button. Also tighten the bottom so it butts up against the footer properly:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: 5 files to replace this round:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Navbar.jsx — H&D removed, just links evenly spaced
Navbar.module.css — bigger font (14px desktop, 11px mobile), fully centered, no monogram
Registry.jsx — now uses the same .page / .page-content wrapper as RSVP and Details
Registry.module.css — styled as a card matching the Details page cards
Home.module.css — padding-top now uses the exact same ticker + navbar calculation as the other pages, mobile title no longer overlaps the scroll button

div.h2: Claude responded: You accidentally pasted the CSS content into Home.

div.p: You accidentally pasted the CSS content into Home.jsx instead of Home.module.css. They're two separate files:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Home.jsx — gets the JSX/JavaScript content
Home.module.css — gets the CSS content

div.p: Open Home.jsx, select all, delete it, and paste the correct JSX from the Home file in the downloads. Then make sure Home.module.css has the CSS content.

div.h2: Claude responded: Both are solvable.

div.p: Both are solvable. Let me think through each properly.
Welcome popup: Use sessionStorage — stores a flag for the browser session so it only shows once per visit, but resets if they close the tab and come back.

Font picker: The real issue is that CSS custom properties set via JavaScript on :root don't force React to re-render components that use them via var() in CSS Modules — because CSS Modules compiles class names at build time and the browser doesn't know to repaint. The fix is to skip CSS variables entirely for the font and instead apply the font directly via inline style on a wrapper element in App.jsx that wraps everything, so React controls it.

div.p: Now fix the font picker properly. The real solution is a React context that passes the font down and applies it via inline styles on a wrapper div — CSS vars alone don't trigger React re-renders:

div.p: Good, h1-h6 already inherit --font-display. The wrapper div with inline CSS vars is the key fix — now React owns the font values and re-renders them on change.

div.p: Two files to replace:

App.jsx — wraps everything in a <div style={fontStyle}> where fontStyle is a React state object containing the CSS variables. Since React owns this object, every font change triggers a real re-render and the variables cascade down to every child element instantly. That's why CSS vars alone weren't working — the browser wasn't getting told to repaint.

Home.jsx — popup now checks sessionStorage.getItem('welcomeSeen') on mount. If it's there, popup never shows. When dismissed, it sets that key. Navigating back to home won't trigger it again for the rest of the session. If they close the browser and come back it'll show once more, which is probably fine.

div.p: Good, I have the full picture. Now I'll rewrite RSVP.jsx with the new structure — each guest entry can have members array, the form renders one attending toggle per member stacked, plus notes field:

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now add the new CSS classes for the attendee rows:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Two files to replace. Here's what changed:

Guest list structure — every guest now has a members array. Chris & Cara are the example with all 5 family members. When you need to add real guests, just follow that pattern — couples get two members, singles get one, families list everyone.
The form — instead of a single "First Name" field, it now renders one stacked row per member, each with their own Attending / Not Attending toggle. All required. Song, allergies, and notes sit below.
Email — sends a formatted summary like:

div.h2: Claude responded: Both fixes — red asterisks via a wrapper span in JSX, and modal z-index pushed above the navbar: Now fix the asterisks in the JSX — wrap the * in a <span class…

div.p: Both fixes — red asterisks via a wrapper span in JSX, and modal z-index pushed above the navbar:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Now fix the asterisks in the JSX — wrap the * in a <span className={styles.required}> in every label:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Two files. Here's what changed:

RSVP.module.css — modal z-index bumped to 1100 (above navbar's 999), overlay now scrolls itself with overflow-y: auto and align-items: flex-start so tall forms like the Gearhart family one scroll naturally without getting clipped by the navbar. .required class is red and slightly larger.
RSVP.jsx — all required field asterisks are now wrapped in * so they render red and bigger. (optional) text was already using .labelNote which is now red too from the last CSS update.

Asterisk — 13px → 16px so it's clearly visible next to the label text

div.h2: Claude responded: EmailJS — it's a free service that lets a static site send emails without a backend.

div.p: EmailJS — it's a free service that lets a static site send emails without a backend. When a guest submits the form it fires directly to hgearhart98@gmail.com.
You still need to set it up. Takes about 5 minutes:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Go to emailjs.com and create a free account
Add a service → connect your Gmail (hgearhart98@gmail.com)
Copy your Service ID, Template ID, and Public Key
Open RSVP.jsx and drop them in at the top:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

div.p: Free tier gives you 200 emails/month which is plenty for a wedding.

div.h2: Claude responded: Formspree is actually a solid alternative — might even be simpler than EmailJS for this use case.

div.p: Formspree is actually a solid alternative — might even be simpler than EmailJS for this use case. Here's the comparison:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Free tier: 50 submissions/month
Setup: create account, make a form, get one endpoint URL, done
No template to configure — just sends you a clean email with all the fields
One line change in the code — swap emailjs.send(...) for a fetch() POST

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Free tier: 200 emails/month
More setup — service, template, public key
More control over how the email looks

div.p: For a wedding with maybe 100-150 guests, 50 submissions is cutting it close on Formspree's free tier. EmailJS's 200 is safer.
That said, Formspree has a $10/month plan with unlimited submissions if you wanted to go that route and keep the code simpler.
My recommendation: stick with EmailJS for the higher free limit. But if you want to switch to Formspree just say the word and I'll swap out the submit logic — it's like a 10 line change.

Once you're in, come back and tell me and I'll walk you through Step 2.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yes — click "Add New Service".

div.p: Yes — click "Add New Service". You'll see a list of email providers. Pick Gmail, then connect it to hgearhart98@gmail.com.
It'll ask you to authorize with Google — go through that flow. Once it says the service is connected, it'll show you a Service ID that looks something like service_xxxxxxx.
Copy that and paste it here, then we'll move to the next step.

Now click "Connect Account" first to link your Gmail, go through the Google auth flow, then click "Create Service".
Once that's done come back and we'll set up the email template.

div.h2: Claude responded: That means it didn't get the right Google permissions.

div.p: That means it didn't get the right Google permissions. Here's the fix:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Click Connect Account again
When the Google popup appears, make sure you check "Send email on your behalf" — that's the permission it needs

If you already clicked through without it, you'll need to disconnect and reconnect the Google account in your Google account settings at myaccount.google.com → Security → Third-party apps and remove EmailJS, then try again

div.p: Go through the Google auth again and make sure that permission is explicitly checked/allowed.

div.h2: Claude responded: Great!

div.p: Great! Now we need to create the email template. In the EmailJS dashboard:

ol.li: Click "Email Templates" in the left sidebar
Click "Create New Template"

div.p: Once you're in the template editor, replace everything with this:

div.p: Once you've pasted that in, click Save. It'll give you a Template ID that looks like template_xxxxxxx.
Paste that here and we'll do the last step.

div.h2: Claude responded: Don't use any of those presets.

div.p: Don't use any of those presets. Just click "Create Template" in the bottom right — that'll create a blank one you can edit yourself. Then paste in the subject and body I gave you in the last message.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yes — when a guest hits Submit RSVP it fires automatically to your Gmail, they don't do anything else.

div.p: Yes — when a guest hits Submit RSVP it fires automatically to your Gmail, they don't do anything else. The email just shows up in your inbox.
Here's what to do on this screen:
Subject field — looks like you already have New RSVP — {{group_name}} in there, perfect.
Content area — click "Edit Content", delete everything in there, and paste this:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre-wrap;

div.p: To Email — already has hgearhart98@gmail.com ✅ leave that alone.
From Name — change {{name}} to just Wedding Website
Reply To — clear out {{email}}, just leave it blank.
Then hit Save in the top right. It'll show you a Template ID like template_xxxxxxx — paste that here and we're almost done.

div.h2: Claude responded: Perfect, that's exactly right!

div.p: Perfect, that's exactly right! Click Apply Changes then Save in the top right.
Once saved, look for the Template ID — it'll be on the template page, something like template_xxxxxxx. Paste it here and we're one step away from being done.

div.p: Just RSVP.jsx. A couple notes on how I parsed the ambiguous ones:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: "Bob Hill Steve Hill" — split into two separate entries since they're likely different people
"Camille & Barclay & Bryan Holt" — treated as 3 members of one family entry
"Todd Allen +1" — listed as Todd + an unnamed guest, you can update the +1 name when you know it
Test Harrison — added at the bottom for testing

div.p: Go submit a test RSVP and make sure the email hits your inbox!

div.h2: Claude responded: Here are 10 options:

div.p: Here are 10 options:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: "Ceremony begins promptly at 4:30 PM. We kindly ask that guests arrive by 4:00 PM. If you arrive after the ceremony has begun, please wait until it has concluded before being seated."
"Please plan to arrive by 4:00 PM — the ceremony starts at 4:30 PM sharp. Out of respect for the couple, late arrivals will be asked to wait until after the ceremony to be seated."
"Doors open at 4:00 PM. The ceremony will begin at 4:30 PM. Guests arriving after 4:30 PM will be warmly welcomed following the ceremony."
"We ask that all guests be seated by 4:15 PM as the ceremony will begin promptly at 4:30 PM. Late arrivals will be seated following the conclusion of the ceremony."
"The ceremony begins at 4:30 PM — please arrive by 4:00 PM to get settled. If you happen to arrive late, we ask that you kindly wait outside until the ceremony has ended."
"Please arrive no later than 4:15 PM. The ceremony will begin promptly at 4:30 PM, and out of respect for Harrison and Daelyn, late arrivals will not be seated until after the ceremony concludes."
"Guests are encouraged to arrive at 4:00 PM. The ceremony begins at 4:30 PM sharp — those arriving after the ceremony has started will be welcomed with open arms once it has concluded."
"Please be seated by 4:15 PM. The ceremony begins at 4:30 PM and will not be delayed. Guests arriving after 4:30 PM will be asked to wait until after the ceremony to join the celebration."
"The ceremony begins at 4:30 PM. To ensure you're comfortable and seated, we suggest arriving around 4:00 PM. Late arrivals are welcome to join us immediately following the ceremony."
"We kindly request all guests arrive by 4:00 PM. The ceremony will begin at 4:30 PM and, to preserve the moment, late arrivals will be seated only after the ceremony has concluded."

div.h2: Claude responded: The FontPicker is imported in App.

div.p: The FontPicker is imported in App.jsx — if the file doesn't exist the whole app crashes. Two options:
Option A — quickest fix: Remove the import and usage from App.jsx. Open it and delete these two lines:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100
sticky opacity-0 group-hover/copy:opacity-100 group-focus-within/copy:opacity-100 top-2 py-2 h-12 w-0 float-right
absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

pre.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono);

code.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: and remove <FontPicker currentFont={displayFont} onFontChange={handleFontChange} /> from the JSX.
Also remove the font state and handler since nothing uses them anymore:

div.class: relative group/copy bg-bg-000/50 border-0.5 border-border-400 rounded-lg focus:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent-100

absolute right-0 h-8 px-2 items-center inline-flex z-10

button.class: inline-flex items-center justify-center relative isolate shrink-0 can-focus select-none disabled:pointer-events-none disabled:opacity-50 disabled:shadow-none disabled:drop-shadow-none border-transparent transition font-base duration-300 ease-[cubic-bezier(0.165,0.85,0.45,1)] h-8 w-8 rounded-md backdrop-blur-md _fill_10ocf_9 _ghost_10ocf_96

div.style: width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
width: 20px; height: 20px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

code.style: color: rgb(20, 24, 31); background: transparent; font-family: var(--font-mono); white-space: pre;

div.p: And the useEffect that applies the fonts, and the fontStyle object and style={fontStyle} on the wrapper div.
Option B — just restore the files if you want to keep the font picker later.
Which do you want? If Option A I can send you a clean App.jsx right now.

Nothing shows until they hit the Search button or press Enter
Only their matching result appears — no other names visible
Clear X resets everything back to blank

div.p: You're right, the fix was only applied to the in-memory file but not saved to outputs properly. Here's the full corrected file:

div.h2: Claude responded: On your Mac, open the Photos app, find the photo you want, then:

div.p: On your Mac, open the Photos app, find the photo you want, then:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Right-click the photo → Share → Save to Files → pick Downloads
Or just drag it straight from Photos onto your Desktop, then move it to Downloads

div.p: Once you have the photos in your Downloads folder, drop them into wedding/src/assets/ in VS Code and I'll give you the exact lines to swap into Home.jsx to wire them up.

div.h2: Claude responded: Try this instead:

div.p: Try this instead:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Open Photos app
Click the photo to open it
At the top menu bar click File → Export → Export 1 Photo
Choose Downloads as the save location
Hit Export

div.p: That'll save it as a JPG to your Downloads folder.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yeah I know — that's the standard Photos app on Mac.

div.p: Yeah I know — that's the standard Photos app on Mac. If File → Export isn't working, try this:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Open the photo in Photos

li.strong: Drag it directly from Photos onto your Desktop

ol.li: Then drag it from Desktop into the src/assets/ folder in VS Code

div.p: That's the easiest way on a personal Mac.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yes, convert to WebP — smaller file size, faster load, better for mobile.

div.p: Yes, convert to WebP — smaller file size, faster load, better for mobile. Run this in your terminal (you already have sharp-cli from the SVS project):

div.h2: Claude responded: Good call — instead of a background-cover that crops, we'll make each slide an actual tag with object-fit: contain so the whole photo is always visible.

div.p: Good call — instead of a background-cover that crops, we'll make each slide an actual tag with object-fit: contain so the whole photo is always visible. It'll letterbox with the dark overlay color on the sides/top if the aspect ratio doesn't fill the screen, which actually looks clean.

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Two files. Each photo now renders as a full with object-fit: contain — the whole picture will always be visible. The dark wine color fills any letterbox areas on the sides or top, which ties in nicely with the color palette.

div.p: Got it. The problem is clear — min-height: 100vh on .hero is forcing scroll, and the .slide still has the old background CSS instead of the img layout. Full rewrite:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.p: Also need to remove the Footer from Home since the page is now position: fixed — the footer nav links are in the navbar anyway:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.p: Both files. Key fixes:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: .home is now position: fixed; inset: 0; overflow: hidden — absolutely no scroll possible
.hero is position: absolute over the slideshow, no min-height nonsense
Photos use object-fit: contain so full image always visible
Footer removed from homepage since it can't scroll anyway — nav links are in the navbar
Everything sized mobile-first with clamp() scaling up for desktop

div.h2: Claude responded: Yes I see the fonts — she wants one of those script/cursive fonts for the display text.

div.p: Yes I see the fonts — she wants one of those script/cursive fonts for the display text. My pick would be Playfair Display — wait, those are all script fonts she sent. For a wedding site I'd go with La Belle Aurore or Petit Formal Script — elegant, not too wild.
Which one does she want, or should I just pick La Belle Aurore?
And on the colors — I'll swap everything out with her palette:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: #f9f3e5 — cream (background)
#e6b0c2 — soft pink
#b87c93 — mauve
#740e45 — deep magenta/plum
#35060e — near black burgundy
#8a8327 — olive gold (accent)

div.p: For readability I'll use the dark #35060e for body text and #740e45 for headings — the light cream background makes those very readable. Sound good or do you want to tweak the pairings?

div.h2: Claude responded: Which one does she want?

div.p: Which one does she want? Or just pick your favorite from the list — they're all script fonts so any of them will look beautiful on a wedding site.
My personal pick: Petit Formal Script — it's elegant and still very readable at large sizes compared to something like Sirivennela which can get hard to read small.
Your call though — just say the word and I'll update everything.

div.p: Now I have everything. Updating index.html with Petit Formal Script, index.css with all new colors, and pushing out all affected files:

div.p: Now update the Navbar and Ticker which use hardcoded dark colors:

div.p: Now update Home, Details, Registry, RSVP module CSS with new colors:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.p: 10 files. Replace all of these. Here's what changed:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: index.html — loads Petit Formal Script from Google Fonts

index.css — full color palette swap to Daelyn's colors. I kept the old variable names (--vintage-wine, --gold etc.) mapped to the new colors so nothing breaks, but also added the proper new names (--burgundy, --plum, --mauve, --olive-gold, --cream, --blush)
All CSS modules — updated to use new color variables

div.p: The cream #f9f3e5 background with #35060e burgundy text will be very readable throughout the site.

div.h2: Claude responded: Looking at this, the issue is the hero text is overlaying the photo which makes it hard to read.

div.p: Looking at this, the issue is the hero text is overlaying the photo which makes it hard to read. Best solution for mobile: split layout — photo on top, text and buttons below on the cream background. Clean, readable, no overlap. On desktop it stays as the full background slideshow.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yeah the navbar links are blending into the photo.

div.p: Yeah the navbar links are blending into the photo. Easy fix — on the home page the navbar is transparent so the links need to be cream/light colored. But since the photo is light/grey toned they're disappearing.
Two options:

ol.li: Give the navbar a solid dark background always on the home page too
Add a subtle dark gradient behind just the navbar area so links are always readable

div.p: Which do you prefer?

div.p: The real issue is .home is position: fixed; inset: 0 but the navbar is also fixed — the photo starts at the very top behind the navbar. The fix is to add padding-top to .home on mobile so the whole layout shifts down below the ticker and navbar:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;

div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: That's the one. The whole home layout now starts exactly below the ticker + navbar so the photo is fully visible and nothing gets cut off.

div.h2: Claude responded: The padding-top pushed the content down making it scroll, and the navbar height is too tall.

div.p: The padding-top pushed the content down making it scroll, and the navbar height is too tall. Let me fix both:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.h2: Claude responded: You already have ScrollToTop in App.

div.p: You already have ScrollToTop in App.jsx which scrolls to the top on page change — but the alignment issue is likely the page jumping because of scrollbar width appearing/disappearing between pages. Add this to index.css to lock it:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Just index.css. The overflow-y: scroll keeps the scrollbar space reserved at all times so the layout width never shifts when switching between pages that scroll and pages that don't.

div.h2: Claude responded: That's a classic mobile issue — when a modal opens it sometimes triggers a zoom or viewport shift on iOS, and when it closes the page doesn't snap back.

div.p: That's a classic mobile issue — when a modal opens it sometimes triggers a zoom or viewport shift on iOS, and when it closes the page doesn't snap back. Fix it by locking the viewport when the modal is open:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Just RSVP.jsx. When the modal opens it locks body to position: fixed; width: 100% which prevents iOS from shifting the viewport. When it closes it releases everything back to normal. That should stop the rightward shift when navigating away.

div.h2: Claude responded: The issue is iOS Safari specifically — when an input inside a modal gets focused, iOS zooms in, and when the modal closes that zoom stays.

div.p: The issue is iOS Safari specifically — when an input inside a modal gets focused, iOS zooms in, and when the modal closes that zoom stays. The fix is to prevent iOS from zooming on input focus by setting the font-size of inputs to at least 16px (iOS only zooms when font-size is below 16px):

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Set input font-size to 16px to prevent iOS auto-zoom on focus

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Set input font-size to 16px to prevent iOS auto-zoom on focus

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Also add this to index.html to tell iOS not to scale the viewport:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Two files. The root cause was iOS Safari auto-zooming when a text input under 16px gets focused — that zoom persists after the modal closes. Two fixes applied:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: RSVP.module.css — all inputs and textareas bumped to font-size: 16px which is the threshold iOS uses to decide whether to zoom
index.html — added maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no to the viewport meta tag to hard-block any scaling

div.h2: Claude responded: The pages have a white #fff hardcoded in the card backgrounds, not using a CSS variable.

div.p: The pages have a white #fff hardcoded in the card backgrounds, not using a CSS variable. Let me fix that:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Four files — every hardcoded #fff is now var(--cream) so it'll use Daelyn's #f9f3e5 everywhere consistently.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yeah good call — the page background is still the old #EFD4C4 almond color.

div.p: Yeah good call — the page background is still the old #EFD4C4 almond color. Let's swap it to the light pink blush #e6b0c2. One line change in index.css:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Just index.css. If #e6b0c2 is too pink we can try the softer #b87c93 mauve or dial it back to the #f9f3e5 cream — just say the word.

div.h2: Claude responded: Looking good!

div.p: Looking good! The mauve background with white cards looks clean. For the subtext color inside the cards — it's currently using --mauve which blends into the background. Let's change it to the burgundy #35060e at a lower opacity so it's clearly readable but still softer than the headings:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Three files. Subtext is now dark burgundy at 60% opacity — readable but still clearly secondary to the headings.

div.h2: Claude responded: Got it — the footer isn't sticking to the bottom, leaving pink space below it.

div.p: Got it — the footer isn't sticking to the bottom, leaving pink space below it. Fix is to make the page layout push the footer to the bottom:

button.class: group/status flex items-center gap-2 py-1 text-sm transition-colors cursor-pointer text-left text-text-500 hover:text-text-300 flex-1 min-w-0

div.style: opacity: 1;
div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: width: 12px; height: 12px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;

div.span: Ran a command, read a file

div.style: grid-template-rows: 0fr;

div.class: row-start-2 col-start-1 relative grid isolate min-w-0

div.p: Just App.css. The .page wrapper now uses min-height: 100vh with display: flex; flex-direction: column and .page-content gets flex: 1 so it stretches to fill all available space, pushing the footer flush to the bottom with no pink showing beneath it.

div.p: My pick: harrisondaelyn.com — short, clean, no numbers, easy for older guests to type.

hr.p: For buying the domain — skip Squarespace for this part. Buy it from:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Namecheap (namecheap.com) — cheapest, usually ~$10-12/year for .com
Cloudflare (cloudflare.com/products/registrar) — at cost pricing, no markup

hr.p: Don't buy it through Squarespace — they charge a premium and it complicates things since you're not hosting on Squarespace anyway.
Once you pick a domain and buy it, the hosting plan is:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Deploy the site on GitHub Pages (free)
Point your domain's DNS to GitHub Pages
Add a CNAME file to the repo

hr.p: Pick a domain name and buy it, then come back and I'll walk you through the GitHub Pages deployment step by step.

div.h2: Claude responded: GitHub Pages absolutely supports custom .

div.p: GitHub Pages absolutely supports custom .com domains — that's what the CNAME file is for. It's free hosting + your own .com. Tons of wedding sites use it.
Step 1 — Buy the domain Go to namecheap.com, search harrisondaelyn.com, buy it. Should be ~$10-13 for the first year.
Step 2 — Deploy to GitHub Pages (free hosting) I'll walk you through this once the domain is bought.
Step 3 — Point the domain to GitHub Pages Takes about 5 minutes in Namecheap's DNS settings.

hr.p: Alternative if you want zero hassle: Netlify (netlify.com) — also free, even easier than GitHub Pages:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Drag and drop your dist folder after running npm run build
Connect your custom domain in one click
Free SSL certificate included

hr.p: Netlify is honestly the easiest option for a static React site. I'd recommend it over GitHub Pages just for simplicity.
My recommendation: Buy domain on Namecheap, host on Netlify. Best combo — cheap domain, free dead-simple hosting.
Want to go that route? Buy the domain first and come back and I'll walk you through Netlify deployment.

div.h2: Claude responded: Actually you're right — GitHub Pages is simpler if you're already using Git.

div.p: Actually you're right — GitHub Pages is simpler if you're already using Git. Here's the full plan:
Step 1 — Buy the domain Go to namecheap.com and buy harrisondaelyn.com now. Come back when that's done.
Step 2 — Push to GitHub Make sure your wedding project is in a GitHub repo. If it's not yet, I'll walk you through that.
Step 3 — Enable GitHub Pages Point it at your repo, done.
Step 4 — Connect Namecheap DNS to GitHub Pages 5 minute DNS config, I'll give you the exact settings to copy paste.
Go grab the domain and come back!

div.h2: Claude responded: Don't add any of those extras.

div.p: Don't add any of those extras. You don't need any of them:

ul.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-disc flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ul.li: Web Hosting — no, GitHub Pages is your free host
WordPress Hosting — no
SSL — no, GitHub Pages gives you free SSL automatically
Premium DNS — no, the free DNS that comes with the domain is fine
Business Email — no
Everything else — no

div.p: Just add harrisondaelyn.com at $11.28/yr to cart and checkout. Nothing else. Come back when it's purchased!

div.h2: Claude responded: Yes!

div.p: Yes! A few options without adding any images:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: Subtle noise/grain — CSS SVG filter that adds a fine grain texture, very elegant
Soft diagonal lines — thin repeating pattern, like a fabric weave
Dot pattern — tiny subtle dots, almost invisible but adds depth
Linen texture — crosshatch pattern that mimics fabric

div.p: My pick for a wedding site: noise/grain — it's the most sophisticated and looks like premium stationery. Very subtle, won't distract from content.
Want me to go ahead with that one?

div.p: Just index.css. It's a pure CSS SVG noise filter — no extra images or packages needed. The opacity is set to 0.08 so it's very subtle, just enough to make the pink background feel like textured paper rather than flat color. If you want it more or less pronounced just let me know and I'll dial it up or down.

div.h2: Claude responded: Yeah makes sense — once a guest submits, they shouldn't show up in search results anymore so they can't accidentally submit twice.

div.p: Yeah makes sense — once a guest submits, they shouldn't show up in search results anymore so they can't accidentally submit twice.

The tricky part is this is a static site with no database. Best approach: use localStorage to store a list of submitted guest IDs on the device. So once someone submits on their phone, their name won't show up again on that same device.
The limitation is it's device-specific — if they search on a different phone it would still show. But for a wedding site that's totally fine, guests will only RSVP once from their own device.
Want me to go ahead with that approach?

div.h2: Claude responded: Yeah exactly, not really worth it for a wedding site.

div.p: Yeah exactly, not really worth it for a wedding site. The better solution would be a real backend/database but that defeats the purpose of keeping it simple and free.

Honestly the simplest approach: just trust EmailJS. You'll see every submission in your inbox. If someone submits twice you'll just have two emails — not a big deal for a wedding of this size. The success message already tells them it was received so most people won't resubmit.
Not worth overcomplicating it. Leave it as is?

div.p: That pushes your built site to a gh-pages branch automatically. Then in your GitHub repo go to Settings → Pages and make sure it's set to deploy from the gh-pages branch.
Do all that and come back — then we'll set up the Namecheap DNS to point to GitHub Pages.

div.p: You're right. Here's the full order:

div.p: Step 2 — Update vite.config.js (set base: '/')
Step 3 — Update package.json (add predeploy/deploy scripts)

div.p: This builds the site and pushes the dist folder to the gh-pages branch automatically. Then go to GitHub → Settings → Pages → set source to gh-pages branch.
Come back once that's done and we'll wire up Namecheap DNS.

div.h2: Claude responded: For a wedding site, classic options:

div.p: For a wedding site, classic options:

ol.class: [li_&]:mb-0 [li_&]:mt-1 [li_&]:gap-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ul]:pb-1 [&:not(:last-child)_ol]:pb-1 list-decimal flex flex-col gap-1 pl-8 mb-3
ol.li: H & D — initials in the script font
💍 — ring emoji
✦ — the gold diamond you use throughout the site
A small heart — simple, romantic
Intertwined H + D monogram — elegant but more complex to make

div.p: My pick: ✦ the gold diamond — it's already your brand element throughout the site, tiny and sharp at favicon size, and looks great in both light and dark browser tabs.
Want me to generate the SVG favicon code for that?

div.p: Good call — personal and fun. I'll make it as an SVG so it scales crisp at all sizes:
